personality_voice.book_baseline: Hagrid is warm, impulsive, fiercely loyal, and emotionally transparent. His speech is phonetically rendered dialect ('yeh', 'ter', 'bin'), he speaks in short declarative bursts, and his emotional reactions are immediate and unguarded. He is gruff but tender, quick to outrage on behalf of others (defending Hermione after 'Mudblood', defending Harry to Dumbledore), and prone to blurting things he shouldn't (the Aragog hint, the pumpkin charm). He has a specific comic texture: his treacle fudge cements Harry's jaw, he jokes about signed photos, he roars at Ron for nearly vomiting on his pumpkins. He is also visibly frightened and destabilized when Fudge and Malfoy arrive - pale, sweating, trembling - which shows emotional vulnerability beneath the physical enormity.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The film preserves the dialect faithfully and consistently. Hagrid's warmth toward Harry is intact (the Knockturn Alley rescue, the slug scene, the hut scenes). His impulsive loyalty is shown when he bursts into Dumbledore's office with the dead rooster. His outrage at 'Mudblood' ('He didn'!') and his reassurance of Hermione are present. His nervous, destabilized state during the Fudge/Malfoy visit is shown through stage directions (sweating, dropping the fruitcake, grabbing the crossbow). The 'follow the spiders' hint is delivered with the same oblique, barely-contained urgency as in the book.
personality_voice.difference: The film loses several small but textured personality moments: the treacle fudge joke and the signed-photos joke are absent, removing a layer of Hagrid's teasing humor. His pride in Hermione ('they haven't invented a spell our Hermione can't do') is present in both but slightly compressed in the film. The book's Hagrid also criticizes Lockhart directly and with relish ('If one word of it was true, I'll eat my kettle'), which is absent from the film - this removes a specific sharpness and irreverence toward authority figures he distrusts. These are secondary traits, not core ones.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality - dialect, warmth, impulsive loyalty, emotional transparency, physical comedy, vulnerability - is preserved with high fidelity. The losses are real but minor: a few jokes, the Lockhart criticism, some comic texture. The dominant pattern of speech and behavior is the same. A score in the 21-22 range is appropriate.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Hagrid serves multiple narrative functions in CoS: (1) rescuer and guide (Knockturn Alley), (2) emotional anchor and safe space for the trio, (3) inadvertent plot driver - his past with Aragog and the Chamber is the key to unlocking the mystery, (4) active defender of Harry and Dumbledore (bursting into the office, confronting Malfoy in Flourish and Blotts, defending Dumbledore during the suspension), (5) provider of the critical clue ('follow the spiders'). In the bookshop fight, Hagrid physically separates Weasley and Malfoy and delivers the moral verdict on the Malfoys. He also initiates the visit to his cabin (the trio come to him), and his absence from the grounds is narratively felt.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: All five functions are present in the film. The Knockturn Alley rescue is intact. The slug scene at his hut is intact. He bursts into Dumbledore's office. He confronts Malfoy in the bookshop (though the physical fight is transferred - see below). He delivers the 'follow the spiders' clue. His absence from the grounds is noted. The film adds a small moment at the end where Hagrid's return to the Great Hall is given emotional weight, which is faithful to the book's feast scene.
narrative_role_agency.difference: In the book, it is Mr. Weasley who physically attacks Malfoy first, and Hagrid who physically separates them. In the film, Mr. Weasley moves to hit Malfoy and Hagrid steps forward to restrain him - this is essentially the same beat, not a transfer. However, the book's Hagrid also delivers a longer moral speech after the fight ('Rotten ter the core, the whole family... bad blood... come on now - let's get outta here'), which the film compresses to two lines. More significantly, the book has Hagrid actively managing the aftermath and shepherding the group away; the film cuts away immediately after his lines. The book's Hagrid also has a scene where he is visibly absent from the grounds and Harry notices this as a wrongness - the film references this but does not dwell on it. These are compressions, not transfers or eliminations.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: All major agency beats are present. The compressions do not reassign Hagrid's functions to other characters. The score sits in the 15-16 range - role and agency are reduced but recognizable, with no significant transfer.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Hagrid's motivations are consistent and clear: loyalty to Harry and the trio, loyalty to Dumbledore, love of magical creatures (including dangerous ones), and a deep fear of being blamed again for something he didn't do. The internal conflict is subtle but present: he knows his past makes him a target, he is terrified of Azkaban, and he is caught between wanting to help Harry and knowing he cannot say too much (the 'follow the spiders' hint is the limit of what he can safely offer). His love of creatures is also a source of tension - he knows Aragog is dangerous but cannot condemn him.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves all of these motivations. His fear of Azkaban is explicit ('Not Azkaban prison'). His loyalty to Dumbledore is shown in his outburst during the suspension scene. His love of creatures is shown through the Aragog backstory (via the memory sequence). The 'follow the spiders' hint shows the same tension between wanting to help and being unable to say more directly.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The film does not show Hagrid's internal conflict about Aragog as richly as the book does. In the book, Harry reflects that Hagrid 'would never have meant to kill anybody' and that he 'probably thought it was a shame that the monster had been cooped up so long' - this is narration, not dialogue, so it cannot appear in the film directly. But the film's memory sequence (young Hagrid defending Aragog) conveys the same emotional truth. The book also has Hagrid's discomfort when the trio visit after learning about the Chamber - he 'hardly seemed to know what he was doing', nearly extinguishing the fire, smashing the teapot - which the film preserves in stage directions.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are simplified slightly (the creature-love tension is less explicit) but not changed or replaced. The core motivations are intact. Score in the 12-13 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Hagrid's arc in CoS is not a transformation arc but a vindication arc: he begins under suspicion (his past with the Chamber), is wrongly imprisoned, and is ultimately cleared and restored. The arc has a clear shape: (1) he is a trusted figure who harbors a secret past, (2) that past is revealed and he is taken away, (3) he leaves a clue that enables the resolution, (4) he is vindicated and returns. This arc is intentionally flat in terms of character change - Hagrid does not grow or change, he is proven innocent. The arc's meaning is about loyalty and false accusation, not personal transformation.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film follows this arc faithfully. All four stages are present. The return to the Great Hall at the end - Hagrid entering nervously, the room going silent, his emotional thanks to Harry - is a faithful rendering of the vindication beat. The film adds a small emotional flourish (the standing ovation) that is consistent with the book's feast scene.
character_arc.difference: The film compresses the middle section - Hagrid's absence from the grounds is less dwelt upon, and the emotional weight of his imprisonment is less developed. In the book, Harry notices the wrongness of the grounds without Hagrid for weeks; the film moves more quickly. But the arc's shape and meaning are intact.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but faithful. All key turning points are present. Score in the 12-13 range.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Hagrid's key relationships in CoS are: (1) Harry - warm, protective, quasi-parental, with Hagrid as a safe harbor and Harry as someone Hagrid will defend at any cost; (2) Dumbledore - deep loyalty and trust, Hagrid looks to Dumbledore for validation and protection; (3) the trio collectively - Hagrid as a trusted adult friend who is also slightly naive about danger; (4) Aragog - a relationship that reveals Hagrid's creature-love and his capacity for long, loyal bonds with non-human beings.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: All four relationships are present and their dynamics are preserved. The Harry-Hagrid dynamic is shown in the Knockturn Alley scene, the hut scenes, and the final Great Hall moment. The Dumbledore-Hagrid loyalty is shown in the suspension scene. The trio relationship is shown in the slug scene and the hut visit. The Aragog relationship is shown through the memory sequence and Aragog's testimony about Hagrid's goodness.
key_relationships.difference: The film slightly reduces the texture of the Harry-Hagrid relationship by cutting the Christmas gift (treacle fudge) and the signed-photos joke, which are small moments of warmth and teasing that build the relationship's intimacy. The Aragog relationship is conveyed primarily through the memory sequence rather than through Harry and Ron's direct conversation with Aragog about Hagrid - the film's Aragog scene is present but slightly compressed.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The emotional dynamics of all key relationships are preserved. The compressions are real but do not change the nature of any relationship. Score in the 7-8 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Hagrid's complexity in CoS comes from several sources: (1) his naivety about danger (Aragog, the pumpkin charm, the Flesh-Eating Slug Repellent) coexisting with genuine wisdom about people; (2) his irreverence toward Lockhart, which shows he is not simply deferential to authority; (3) his comic texture (treacle fudge, signed photos, roaring at Ron over the pumpkins); (4) his vulnerability when threatened (the crossbow, the pale sweating during the Fudge visit); (5) the pathos of his situation - a man whose past makes him perpetually suspect, who is innocent but cannot fully prove it, who is loved by those who know him and feared by those who don't.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film preserves the vulnerability, the loyalty, the naivety about danger (the 'follow the spiders' hint), and the pathos of his situation. The Lockhart criticism is absent, removing one layer of his irreverence. The comic texture is reduced (no treacle fudge joke, no signed-photos joke, no roaring at Ron over the pumpkins). The pumpkin charm scene is entirely absent, which removes a specific instance of his rule-bending creature-love and his slightly guilty relationship with his own expelled status.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The pumpkin scene is a meaningful loss: it shows Hagrid using magic illegally, being caught by Hermione, and being slightly sheepish about it - a moment that complicates his relationship with rules and his own history. Its absence removes a nuance. The Lockhart criticism is also a loss - it shows Hagrid has sharp judgment about people, not just creatures. These are not catastrophic losses but they are real ones.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The character remains substantially complete. The losses are real but secondary - they reduce texture without eliminating any core trait or function. Score in the 11-12 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Pumpkin Engorgement Charm scene - Hagrid's illegal magic, his sheepishness, and the comic texture of the moment are absent
Lockhart criticism ('If one word of it was true, I'll eat my kettle') - removes Hagrid's sharp irreverence toward fraudulent authority
Treacle fudge joke and signed-photos joke - removes a layer of teasing humor and warmth in the Harry-Hagrid relationship
Roaring at Ron over the pumpkins - a small but characteristic comic beat absent from the film
Christmas gift of treacle fudge - a small intimacy marker in the Harry-Hagrid relationship
Extended moral speech after the Flourish and Blotts confrontation is compressed
The sustained narrative of Hagrid's absence from the grounds and Harry's sense of wrongness is compressed
key_observations: The film's Hagrid is a faithful and largely complete adaptation. The dialect, warmth, impulsive loyalty, emotional transparency, and narrative functions are all preserved. The losses are real but secondary: comic texture, a few jokes, the pumpkin scene, and the Lockhart criticism reduce nuance without altering the character's core identity, motivations, arc, or key relationships. No major agency is transferred to another character. The vindication arc is intact. The score of 83 reflects a high-fidelity adaptation with minor but genuine simplification of the character's texture and secondary traits.
